scenario: A FEEL expression uses today() for date calculations; another uses now(). The team wonders about the difference. today() vs now() in FEEL -- when to use which?
question: today() vs now() in FEEL -- when to use which?

options.text: They return different types and serve different purposes. today() returns a date value containing only the year, month, and day components without any time or timezone information. now() returns a date and time value containing the year, month, day, hour, minute, second, and timezone. Using today() where now() is expected, or vice versa, will produce type mismatches in comparisons and arithmetic. Expressions that compare a date value against a date and time value behave differently from comparing two values of the same type, and mixing them can produce unexpected results.

today() returns the current date value (date only, no time component). , 2026-05-14. Useful for date arithmetic where time-of-day doesn't matter. now() returns the current date and time value (date + time + zone). , 2026-05-14T09:30:00Z. Useful for timestamps, precise moments, time-of-day-aware logic. ").

The today() function in FEEL returns a value of type date, which represents only year, month, and day. It has no hour, minute, second, or timezone component. Attempting to access a time property on the result of today() returns null. When a FEEL expression needs the current date-and-time with precision to the second and with timezone context, now() must be used instead. Using today() in a context that requires time information produces incorrect or null values without a clear error signal.

The now() function in FEEL returns a date and time value that includes year, month, day, hour, minute, second, and timezone information. It provides a complete temporal snapshot of the current moment. Using now() in a context that only needs a date comparison (such as dueDate < today()) would work for same-day comparisons in some cases but would be semantically imprecise and type-inconsistent, as comparing a date with a date and time produces different behavior than comparing two values of the same type. now() is the correct choice when sub-day precision is required.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL's "current moment" functions differ in return type: today(): Returns: date type -- year, month, day only. Example: today() = date("2026-05-14"). Use when: date-only comparisons, day-level arithmetic. Examples: =dueDate now() -- is event happening right now? Choosing: Date-only logic (deadlines by date, age in years, calendar comparisons): today(). Time-of-day or duration-based (latency, freshness, "happening now"): now(). Implicit conversion: today() can be combined with time(...) to get a date-and-time at a specific time of day. now() can be converted to date via date(now()) -- strips time.
